name: Bohrium
name_origin: Named after Danish physicist Niels Bohr, a foundational figure in quantum theory and atomic structure.

discovery.discoverer: Gesellschaft fur Schwerionenforschung (GSI) team led by Peter Armbruster and Gottfried Munzenberg

occurrence.abundance_summary: Bohrium does not occur naturally. It is a synthetic element produced in particle accelerators by cold fusion reactions such as 54Cr on 209Bi.
states: synthetic, produced atom-by-atom in particle accelerators

compounds.name: Bohrium oxychloride
compounds.summary: A volatile oxychloride observed in gas-phase chromatography experiments, analogous to TcO3Cl and ReO3Cl, confirming bohrium's classification as a group 7 element.

reactivity_summary: Bohrium behaves as a group 7 transition metal, chemically resembling rhenium and technetium with a dominant +7 oxidation state. Gas-phase experiments demonstrate it forms volatile oxychlorides similar to those of its lighter homologs, with slightly lower volatility than ReO3Cl.
isotopes_summary: All bohrium isotopes are radioactive. The longest-lived confirmed isotope is 270Bh with a half-life of about 1 minute; shorter-lived isotopes such as 267Bh (half-life ~17 seconds) have been used in single-atom chemistry experiments.

citations.source: IUPAC Periodic Table of the Elements
Royal Society of Chemistry - Periodic Table: Bohrium
Eichler, R. et al. 'Chemical characterization of bohrium (element 107)', Nature, 2000
